perturbation.original_text: Distributor agrees not to represent or sell other products which are deemed to be competitive with the Company's Products unless agreed to by the Company by written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor agrees not to represent or sell other products which are deemed to be competitive with the Company's Products, with 'competitive' being at the sole discretion of the distributor, unless agreed to by the Company by written notice.
perturbation.explanation: By giving the distributor the sole discretion to decide what is "competitive", the definition of "competitive products" becomes ambiguous and potentially conflicts with typical understandings in antitrust law. This could lead to disputes over whether the distributor is fulfilling their obligation to market the Company's products exclusively.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sherman Act prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. This clause, by giving the distributor sole discretion over defining 'competitive,' could be interpreted as a way to circumvent antitrust regulations if the distributor uses it to stifle competition unfairly. The law requires a reasonable and justifiable definition of what constitutes restraint of trade, and the contract contradicts this by leaving it undefined.

Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the distributor to unilaterally define what constitutes a 'competitive' product, potentially undermining the original intent of preventing the distributor from selling competing products. This introduces ambiguity that could allow the distributor to circumvent restrictions. The Law Explanation states this could be interpreted as circumventing antitrust regulations if the distributor uses it to stifle competition unfairly, as the law requires a reasonable and justifiable definition of what constitutes restraint of trade, and the contract contradicts this by leaving it undefined. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal." The change creates a legal contradiction because it deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable, as the distributor's sole discretion could enable them to effectively nullify the non-compete clause, contradicting the intent to prevent restraint of trade.

perturbation.original_text: Company agrees to ship units to Distributor as close as possible to the delivery schedule set forth in each order as accepted by Company, unless Company otherwise indicates in writing
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees to attempt shipment to Distributor, and the delivery schedule is non-binding and subject to change without notice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the delivery schedule to be non-binding and subject to change without notice weakens the obligation of the Company.  This conflicts with established contract law regarding reasonable expectations and good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-309 (Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination)
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 states that when a contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration, it is valid for a reasonable time. Further, termination of a contract by one party requires reasonable notification be received by the other party. The modified text makes delivery schedules non-binding and allows changes without notice, which contradicts the UCC's requirement for reasonable notification, potentially making the agreement unenforceable or creating a breach of contract situation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by making delivery schedules non-binding and subject to change without notice, directly contradicts the principle of 'reasonable time' as mandated by UCC § 2-309. Snippet 1 explicitly states that 'the time for shipment or delivery... if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time.' The original text committed to shipping 'as close as possible' to the agreed schedule. The changed text removes this commitment, substituting it with a non-binding schedule subject to change, violating the reasonableness standard and establishing a legal contradiction because contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions.

perturbation.original_text: Company warrants that Distributor shall acquire Products purchased hereunder free and clear of all liens and encumbrances except for Company's purchase money security interest defined in Section 1.03, above. Company further warrants all Products to be free from defects in material or workmanship under normal use and service for a period of ninety (90) days from the date of delivery.
perturbation.changed_text: Company warrants that Distributor shall acquire Products purchased hereunder free and clear of most liens and encumbrances except for Company's purchase money security interest defined in Section 1.03, above. Company warrants all Products to be generally free from major defects in material or workmanship under typical use and maintenance for approximately three months from the date of delivery.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "all liens" to "most liens", "free from defects" to "generally free from major defects", "normal use and service" to "typical use and maintenance", and "ninety (90) days" to "approximately three months" significantly weakens the warranty. The addition of major and typical introduces ambiguity, while also weakening the warranty and being approximate is not specific. This creates a contradiction because the term major is not defined.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-313 (Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample)
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-313 governs express warranties in sales contracts. The modified text introduces vague terms like 'most liens,' 'generally free from major defects,' 'typical use,' and 'approximately three months.' These terms undermine the clear and unequivocal warranty that the UCC requires, potentially nullifying the warranty due to vagueness and lack of specificity. By not clearly defining the extent and duration of the warranty, the modified text contradicts the principles of express warranty as outlined in the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principles of express warranty under UCC § 2-313. The original text offers a clear warranty against "all liens and encumbrances" and "defects in material or workmanship." The changed text weakens this warranty by using ambiguous terms like "most liens," "generally free from major defects," "typical use," and "approximately three months." These terms introduce vagueness and uncertainty, potentially nullifying the warranty because they are not clearly defined as stated in UCC § 2-313 (1)(a): "Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise."
